steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure around the blank
steps.content:
The sentence contains a between X and Y construction: “researchers studying connections between [the physical location in which a social media post was created]” and “[the post's content]” analyzed geotagged social media posts.” We're coordinating two separate items: the location where a post originated and the post's content.

On the left side of the blank, we complete the first item; after the blank, we introduce the second item with “and the post's content.”

The structural question is whether commas are needed around “and the post's content” in this two-item coordination.

steps.title: Apply the between…and rule
steps.content:
In a between X and Y construction with exactly two items, we do not insert a comma immediately before “and” (the Oxford comma applies only to lists of three or more items).

We also do not place a comma after Y unless the sentence structure independently requires one (such as a parenthetical interruption).

Here, there is no such interruption—the verb “analyzed” immediately follows.

The correct form is simply: “created and the post's content” with zero commas.

Adding a comma before “and” is incorrect; adding a comma after the object unnecessarily separates the object from the verb; using both commas doubles the error.

That matches Choice A — created and the post's content.
